item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Coworking Open House Week @ The Cloud Room
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: The Cloud Room, 1424 11th Ave Suite 400, Seattle, WA

Curious about coworking and looking for the perfect spot to get work done and reach your goals? Join us for a complimentary day during our upcoming Open House Week and experience what it’s like to be a part of our incredible community! What's Included: One Free Trial Day: Pick any day during Open House Week to try out our coworking space, free of charge! Arrive anytime 9am or later — whatever works best with your schedule. Access to our beautiful workspace including quiet desk space with monitors, large lounge area, phone booths, bar, and outdoor patio. Complimentary coffee, tea, snacks and kombucha. High-speed Wi-Fi, printing, and meeting rooms available for rent The chance to meet like-minded professionals and get a feel for the Cloud Room vibe. Special Offer: If you love your free day with us and decide to become a member, we’ll waive your initiation fees when you sign up! How to Join: Reserve your free trial day by selecting one day of your choice on our Calendly \(one free trial day per person\) Show up and enjoy your day at Cloud Room! Be sure to check in with the staff at the front of the lounge so we can give you a quick tour of the space and get you set up with the wifi. Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! Current Promotions: ⭐️ Sign up for a grounded membership and get your first month free \+ 10% off you first year of membership. 3 month commitment required. ⭐️ Floating Membership Promo: Take 50% off your first full month of floating membership. Spaces are limited—RSVP today.

item.title: Spy Maters Camp II
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Location: Suncatcher Forest School, 18701 se 60th st, Issaquah, WA

Calling all young secret agents! This time kids are welcome to join action-packed Outdoor Spy Camp, where kids will train to become top-notch spies through thrilling challenges, daring missions, and teamwork! Obstacle Courses & Ninja Training – Agents-in-training will test their agility, speed, and stealth as they navigate exciting ninja courses, crawl through the forest and learn to use nature objects to complete the challenge. Scavenger Hunts & Riddle Solving – Spies must sharpen their detective skills by decoding secret messages, solving riddles, and uncovering hidden clues to complete their top-secret missions. Nerf Battles & Tactical Challenges – Gear up for friendly Nerf wars, where teamwork and strategy are key to outsmarting the opposition. Agents will also practice their precision and accuracy with target practice and stealth maneuvers. Escape Room-Style Puzzles – Secret agents will work together to crack codes, unlock treasure chests, and solve mind-bending puzzles before time runs out! Outdoor Adventure & Camouflage Training – Spies will blend into their surroundings, practice silent movement, and use nature to their advantage while completing their missions. This high-energy camp is perfect for kids who love adventure, problem-solving, and action-packed fun in the great outdoors! Are you ready to accept the mission? - follow the link to book a spot!

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Free Tax Help
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 11 AM

Monday, April 13, 2026, 11am - 5pm Trained volunteers will be available to answer questions and prepare personal tax returns. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 5 - Mixing Chamber Meeting Room Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: Drop-in tax help is offered on a first-come, first-served basis., This service is free and available to individuals only., The free service is not available for business tax returns., To receive tax help, bring the following documents:, Required Documents: Photo identification, Social Security cards or Individual Taxpayer Identification Numbers \(ITIN\) for everyone listed on the tax return, Proof of income, for example, W2s and 1099s \(Social Security benefits\), Official documents on Social Security Administration letterhead, Medicare card \(if Social Security number is followed by an "A"\), Be prepared to provide information about health care coverage for each person on the return, Recommended: Last year's tax return, if available, Bank account and routing numbers for direct deposit of refunds, Photocopies or computer print-outs of any of these documents will be accepted., Due to federal budget cuts the IRS is unable to provide the Library with certain tax forms and instruction booklets., Most IRS forms are available online at www.irs.gov. , You may make free black-and-white prints of the forms from Library computers: www.spl.org/Print , Thanks to our partner United Way of King County . Event Logistics: United Way Tax Help locations serve anyone who earned less than $80,000 in 2025 \(or less than $96,000 for non-single filers\)., For eligibility details, contact United Way at 206-461-3700. Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. ADA Accommodations: We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations., All Library programs are free and open to the public.,

item.title: AI Practitioner Part 1: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: MAKERS, 92 Lenora Street, Seattle, WA

Monday, April 13, 2026, 1 - 4pm In this AI foundational 6-part series, you will learn fundamental concepts and terminology related to artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 4 - Room 4 Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: Explore the relationships between these different AI approaches and understand how to distinguish scenarios suited for traditional software methods versus AI/ML techniques., This session covers key AWS AI services and their capabilities, including Amazon SageMaker, Amazon Bedrock, and pre-trained AI services. You will be introduced to foundation models, how they work, and important considerations when using them, such as potential risks and strategies for optimizing outputs. By the end of the session, you will have a broad understanding of AI concepts, AWS tools for AI development, and foundational knowledge to continue learning about generative AI and responsible AI practices., In this session, you will learn to: Define artificial intelligence and machine learning terminology, Explain the relationship between the concepts of AI, machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI, Distinguish between scenarios that are suited for traditional software methods, traditional ML methods, or generative AI, Identify the capabilities of AWS managed AI/ML services, Explain how tokens, vectors, and embeddings are used in a generative AI model, Describe the effect of model parameters on model outputs, Discuss the factors to consider when selecting a foundation model, This session is intended for one or more of the following: Individuals who are ready to learn about AWS AI related technologies, Individuals in both technical and non-technical roles, Individuals looking reskill for the AWS Cloud, Business decision makers, It is recommended that you have the following general skills: Understanding of AWS global infrastructure, Understanding of AWS networking services and how to build a functioning Amazon VPC solution, Understanding of Amazon S3, Amazon EBS, and Amazon EFS, Understanding of AWS security services, Understanding of virtual computer services using Amazon EC2, These skills can be gained by taking the following AWS Skills Center session: AWS Skills Centers: Becoming a Cloud Practitioner - Parts 1-4, Thanks to our partner AWS Skills Center Seattle . Event Logistics: The program aligns with concepts covered in the AWS Certified AI Practitioner exam but is designed as an introductory overview rather than exam preparation. The classes are led by AWS Certified Instructors and in partnership with the AWS Skills Centers . Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. ADA Accommodations: We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations., All Library programs are free and open to the public.,

item.title: Cooking from Scratch: Skills, Techniques & Recipes
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Learn how to cook like a pro! Build your confidence in the kitchen while mastering essential cooking methods and techniques.  Whether you’re just starting out or already love to cook, this fun, hands-on class will help you master the building blocks of great cooking. Learn how to handle a knife with confidence, prepare proteins, and turn fresh vegetables and hearty starches into flavorful dishes. Explore the secrets behind rich stocks and silky sauces, as well as the magic of hydrocolloids \(for thickening and gelling\).  Discover how to balance flavors with finesse, work efficiently with smart kitchen mechanics, and keep things safe and clean with proper sanitation. Come sharpen your skills, boost your confidence, and enjoy the satisfaction of creating meals that truly shine. Learning Objectives Cooking methods and skills Knife skills  Working with proteins, vegetables and starches Preparing stocks and sauces, and using hydrocolloids Balancing flavors Kitchen mechanics, sanitation and safety  Who Might Enjoy This Class? Home cooks who would like to cook better, diversify what they make, get better results, and be more efficient with their time in the kitchen  Anyone ready to learn cooking fundamentals and improve their skills  Competent cooks and professionals wanting to fill in gaps in their skills and learn from a pro

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Event details

Detailed Description: Lớp Học Chuẩn Bị Thi Quốc Tịch Trực Tuyến - Tiếng Việt & Tiếng AnhLớp học vào các ngày Thứ Ba và Thứ Năm trong 11tuần, bắt đầu từ 4/7/2026 và kết thúc vào 6/18/2026. Các lớp học gặp mặt trực tiếp tại ACRS; Các lớp học thứ năm là ảo. Người dạy là một giảng viên giàu kinh nghiệm, giảng dạy bằng cả tiếng Việt và tiếng Anh., Lớp học này được tạo ra dành cho những người cần giúp đỡ để chuẩn bị cho bài thi quốc tịch của họ.; lớp được cung cấp bởi tổ chức Asian Counseling and Referral Service ., ---------------------------------------, The class meets Tuesdays and Thursdays for 11 weeks, starting on April 7 and ending on June 18. Tuesday classes meet in-person at ACRS; Thursday classes are virtual. It is taught by an experienced instructor in Vietnamese and English., This class is designed for individuals that need assistance in preparing for their citizenship exam; the class is provided by Asian Counseling and Referral Service., Thanks to our partner Asian Counseling and Referral Service . Event Logistics: Bắt buộc phải đăng ký để tham gia. Hãy bấm vào đây để đăng ký., Location: 3639 Martin Luther King Jr Way S \#106, Seattle, WA 98108 , Học viên phải nộp bản sao thẻ xanh để ghi danh theo học lớp này., Để tham gia vào lớp học này, quý vị cần có một kết nối mạng internet và một thiết bị để vào mạng internet \(điện thoại thông minh, máy laptop, máy tính bàn, hoặc máy tính bảng\) với phần mềm gọi họp qua mạng Microsoft Teams được cài đặt sẵn, và quý vị sẽ cần một địa chỉ email để đăng ký tham gia., Nếu quý vị cần trợ giúp trong việc tạo một địa chỉ email hoặc cài đặt phần mềm Microsoft Teams, xin liên lạc với bộ phận trợ giúp của tổ chức TechConnect Washington Community trước khi lớp học bắt đầu. Trợ giúp về công nghệ có sẵn trong các ngôn ngữ: tiếng Afaan Oromoo, tiếng Amharic, tiếng Anh, tiếng Sidamo, tiếng Tây Ban Nha, tiếng Tigrinya, tiếng Việt, và tiếng Wolaytta, 7 ngày trong một tuần từ 8h sáng đến 8h tối bằng điện thoại 1-800-216-1132 , đối thoại qua mạng \(web chat\), và qua email tại địa chỉ techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Tìm hiểu thêm về Hỗ trợ công dân. , ---------------------------------------, Registration is required.Register here ., Asian Counseling and Referral Service 3639 Martin Luther King Jr Way S \#106, Seattle, WA 98108 , Students are required to submit a copy of their green card in order to register for this class., , To take part in the class you will need an internet connection and a device to connect to the internet \(smartphone, laptop, desktop, or tablet computer\) with the Microsoft Teams web/video conferencing platform installed, and you will need an email address to register., If you need help getting an email address or installing or using Microsoft Teams, contact the TechConnect Washington Community helpdesk before the class is scheduled to begin., Technical support is available in Afaan Oromoo, Amharic, English, Sidamo, Spanish, Tigrinya, Vietnamese, and Wolaytta, 7 days a week between 8 am - 8 pm by phone \(800\) 216-1132 , web chat , and email to techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Find out more about Citizenship Help . Contact Us: Nếu quý vị có thắc mắc về lớp học, hãy liên hệ với giảng viên Duy Ngo qua email duyn@acrs.org hoặc gọi \(206\) 705-8186.---------------------------------------, If you have questions about the class, contact the instructor Duy Ngo via email at duyn@acrs.org , call or textmessage \(206\) 705-8186. item.title: Coworking Open House Week @ The Cloud Room
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: The Cloud Room, 1424 11th Ave Suite 400, Seattle, WA

Curious about coworking and looking for the perfect spot to get work done and reach your goals? Join us for a complimentary day during our upcoming Open House Week and experience what it’s like to be a part of our incredible community! What's Included: One Free Trial Day: Pick any day during Open House Week to try out our coworking space, free of charge! Arrive anytime 9am or later — whatever works best with your schedule. Access to our beautiful workspace including quiet desk space with monitors, large lounge area, phone booths, bar, and outdoor patio. Complimentary coffee, tea, snacks and kombucha. High-speed Wi-Fi, printing, and meeting rooms available for rent The chance to meet like-minded professionals and get a feel for the Cloud Room vibe. Special Offer: If you love your free day with us and decide to become a member, we’ll waive your initiation fees when you sign up! How to Join: Reserve your free trial day by selecting one day of your choice on our Calendly \(one free trial day per person\) Show up and enjoy your day at Cloud Room! Be sure to check in with the staff at the front of the lounge so we can give you a quick tour of the space and get you set up with the wifi. Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! Current Promotions: ⭐️ Sign up for a grounded membership and get your first month free \+ 10% off you first year of membership. 3 month commitment required. ⭐️ Floating Membership Promo: Take 50% off your first full month of floating membership. Spaces are limited—RSVP today.

item.title: Coworking Tuesdays
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Location: Bothell Kenmore Chamber, 10031 Main Street, Suite A, Bothell, WA

Tuesday Cowork \+ Coffee/Tea \(Weekly\) Need a reason to get out of your inbox cave and into a productive, low-pressure space? Join us every Tuesday for a drop-in coworking morning—bring your laptop, knock out your priorities, and enjoy the energy of working alongside other local professionals. When: Tuesdays | 9:30–11:30 AM | No registration needed Format: Come for the full two hours or pop in when you can Who it’s for: Business owners, remote workers, creatives, students, nonprofit leaders—anyone who wants focused work time \+ light community What to expect: Quiet “get-it-done” work blocks, optional hellos \+ quick intros, and a friendly vibe \(no forced networking\) Location \(through February\): Bothell Visitor Center We’ll rotate locations monthly—same time, same Tuesday rhythm, fresh spaces. Bring: Your laptop \+ whatever you need to work. We have the coffee, tea, and water! Questions? Email sandra@bothellkenmorechamber.org

Event details

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Location Link: Ballard Branch Room Location: Meeting Room Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: This service is free and available to individuals only. The free service is not available for business tax returns. To receive tax help, bring the following documents:, Required: Photo identification, Social Security cards or Individual Taxpayer Identification Numbers \(ITIN\) for everyone listed on the tax return., Proof of income, for example, W2s and 1099s \(Social Security benefits\), Official documents on Social Security Administration letterhead, Medicare card \(if Social Security number is followed by an "A"\), Be prepared to provide information about health care coverage for each person on the return., Recommended: Last year's tax return, if available, Bank account and routing numbers for direct deposit of refunds, Photocopies or computer print-outs of any of these documents will be accepted.Original documents highly recommended., Due to federal budget cuts the IRS is unable to provide the Library with certain tax forms and instruction booklets., Most IRS forms are available online at www.irs.gov ., You may make free black-and-white prints of the forms from Library computers: www.spl.org/Print , Thanks to our partner AARP . item.title: Low Vision Book Group: "West with Giraffes"
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 11:30 AM

Tuesday, April 14, 2026, 11:30am - 1pm Join our Low Vision Book Club for a discussion of "West with Giraffes" by Lynda Rutledge. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 4 - Room 6 Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: About the book:, Woodrow Wilson Nickel, age 105, feels his life ebbing away. But when he learns giraffes are going extinct, he finds himself recalling the unforgettable experience he cannot take to his grave. It's 1938. The Great Depression lingers. Hitler is threatening Europe, and world-weary Americans long for wonder. They find it in two giraffes who miraculously survive a hurricane while crossing the Atlantic. What follows is a twelve-day road trip in a custom truck to deliver Southern California's first giraffes to the San Diego Zoo. Behind the wheel is the young Dust Bowl rowdy Woodrow. Inspired by true events, the tale weaves real-life figures with fictional ones, including the world's first female zoo director, a crusty old man with a past, a young female photographer with a secret, and assorted reprobates as spotty as the giraffes. Part adventure, part historical saga, and part coming-of-age love story, West with Giraffes explores what it means to be changed by the grace of animals, the kindness of strangers, the passing of time, and a story told before it's too late. Event Logistics: View in catalog: " West With Giraffes " byLyndaRutledge. Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. Sự kiện này sẽ được diễn giải trực tiếp sang español \(Spanish\), tiếng Việt \(Vietnamese\) và af Soomali \(Somali\). ------------------------------------------ Wadahalada Hal-abuurka leh ee Bulshada dhexdeeda Nagala soo qaybgal kulan farshaxan iyo kheyraad bulsho. Dhacdadaan ayaa si toos ah lagurjumi doonaa español \(Spanish\), tiếng Việt \(Vietnamese\) iyo af Soomaali \(Somali\). item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Special Offer: If you love your free day with us and decide to become a member, we’ll waive your initiation fees when you sign up! How to Join: Reserve your free trial day by selecting one day of your choice on our Calendly \(one free trial day per person\) Show up and enjoy your day at Cloud Room! Be sure to check in with the staff at the front of the lounge so we can give you a quick tour of the space and get you set up with the wifi. Sign up for membership to take advantage of waived initiation fees. Don’t miss this chance to test out our space, meet amazing people, and see if Cloud Room is the perfect fit for your work needs! Current Promotions: ⭐️ Sign up for a grounded membership and get your first month free \+ 10% off you first year of membership. 3 month commitment required. ⭐️ Floating Membership Promo: Take 50% off your first full month of floating membership. Spaces are limited—RSVP today.

This service is free and available to individuals only. The free service is not available for business tax returns. To receive tax help, bring the following documents:, Required Documents: Photo identification, Social Security cards or Individual Taxpayer Identification Numbers \(ITIN\) for everyone listed on the tax return., Proof of income, for example, W2s and 1099s \(Social Security benefits\), Official documents on Social Security Administration letterhead, Medicare card \(if Social Security number is followed by an "A"\), Be prepared to provide information about health care coverage for each person on the return., Recommended: Last year's tax return, if available, Bank account and routing numbers for direct deposit of refunds, Photocopies or computer print-outs of any of these documents willbe accepted., Due to federal budget cuts the IRS is unable to provide the Library with certain tax forms and instruction booklets., Most IRS forms are available online at www.irs.gov ., You may make free black-and-white prints of the forms from Library computers: www.spl.org/Print , Thanks to our partner AARP . item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Trợ giúp về công nghệ có sẵn trong các ngôn ngữ: tiếng Afaan Oromoo, tiếng Amharic, tiếng Anh, tiếng Sidamo, tiếng Tây Ban Nha, tiếng Tigrinya, tiếng Việt, và tiếng Wolaytta, 7 ngày trong một tuần từ 8h sáng đến 8h tối bằng điện thoại 1-800-216-1132 , đối thoại qua mạng \(web chat\), và qua email tại địa chỉ techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Tìm hiểu thêm về Hỗ trợ công dân. , ---------------------------------------, Registration is required.Register here ., Students are required to submit a copy of their green card in order to register for this class., , To take part in the class you will need an internet connection and a device to connect to the internet \(smartphone, laptop, desktop, or tablet computer\) with the Microsoft Teams web/video conferencing platform installed, and you will need an email address to register., If you need help getting an email address or installing or using Microsoft Teams, contact the TechConnect Washington Community helpdesk before the class is scheduled to begin., Technical support is available in Afaan Oromoo, Amharic, English, Sidamo, Spanish, Tigrinya, Vietnamese, and Wolaytta, 7 days a week between 8 am - 8 pm by phone \(800\) 216-1132 , web chat , and email to techconnect@eec-wa.org ., Find out more about Citizenship Help . Contact Us: Nếu quý vị có thắc mắc về lớp học, hãy liên hệ với giảng viên Duy Ngo qua email duyn@acrs.org hoặc gọi \(206\) 705-8186.---------------------------------------, If you have questions about the class, contact the instructor Duy Ngo via email at duyn@acrs.org , call or textmessage \(206\) 705-8186. item.title: Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State Exhibit
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Northwest Worklofts (Commons), 107 West Denny Way, Seattle, WA

Thursday, April 16, 2026, 10am - 6pm This exhibitconfronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 8 - Gallery Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State" confronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. The project leverages multimedia storytelling and community conversations to raise awareness about and advocate for an end to crippling court-imposed costs, fees, fines, and restitution, commonly referred to as Legal Financial Obligations, or LFOs. "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State," goes beyond polarizing headlines and statistics, shining a light on this injustice by sharing stories of our common humanity., Thanks to our partner Living with Conviction ., Thanks to our sponsor The Seattle Public Library Foundation . Event Logistics: The gallery is open during Central Libray's open hours., Living with Conviction is a 501\(c\)\(3\) nonprofit that works to secure economic and racial justice with and by marginalized communities, especially formerly incarcerated, through community-driven research, storytelling, and legal empowerment strategies., This exhibit runs through July 26, 2026, Learn more about Exhibits . Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. ADA Accommodations: We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State Exhibit
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Friday, April 17, 2026, 10am - 6pm This exhibitconfronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. Location Link: Central Library Room Location: Level 8 - Gallery Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State" confronts how Washington courts have been sentencing people not only to prison but also to a lifetime of debt. The project leverages multimedia storytelling and community conversations to raise awareness about and advocate for an end to crippling court-imposed costs, fees, fines, and restitution, commonly referred to as Legal Financial Obligations, or LFOs. "Living with Conviction: Sentenced to Debt for Life in Washington State," goes beyond polarizing headlines and statistics, shining a light on this injustice by sharing stories of our common humanity., Thanks to our partner Living with Conviction ., Thanks to our sponsor The Seattle Public Library Foundation . Event Logistics: The gallery is open during Central Libray's open hours., Living with Conviction is a 501\(c\)\(3\) nonprofit that works to secure economic and racial justice with and by marginalized communities, especially formerly incarcerated, through community-driven research, storytelling, and legal empowerment strategies., This exhibit runs through July 26, 2026, Learn more about Exhibits . item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. item.title: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Amazon Frontier, 2205 7th Ave, Seattle, WA

Experience the Central District Community Preservation and Development Authority \(CDCPDA\) benefit brunch: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance. Now in its second year, this annual gathering brings together more than 200 visionaries—professionals, philanthropists, policymakers, and community leaders—united by a shared commitment to advancing Black economic empowerment in Seattle’s Central District and beyond.  Anchored in the themes: Collaborate. Sustain. Thrive. The brunch creates a dynamic space for meaningful connection, shared learning, and forward-looking action. The program will highlight current tenants, key partnerships, and community activations, while also providing important updates on the progress of the McKinney Center for Community and Economic Development—and a look ahead at what’s to come for the future of this vital project. Guests will engage directly with CDCPDA staff and board members, as well as tenants of the McKinney Center, a cornerstone community hub driving inclusive economic growth. In partnership with Amazon’s Black Employee Network, this event moves beyond celebration to the intentional cultivation of Black excellence and long-term community sustainability. Join us for a meaningful experience that goes beyond traditional networking—one rooted in collaboration, resilience, and collective progress. Your presence helps fuel transformative impact and strengthens the foundation for a thriving, equitable future. Attendee | $150.00﻿ ﻿Full access to the brunch, keynote, and networking. Your ticket directly supports the McKinney Center and community-rooted development in the Central District. Vendor Table | $175.00 Table your business or organization during the first hour of the event. Includes one attendee ticket. Full Table \(8 seats\) | $1,200.00 Bring your team or clients. Includes 8 individual tickets — a savings of $1,000 vs. individual pricing. Guest details can be submitted after purchase. Be part of the movement. Together, we spring forward—collaborating, sustaining, and thriving. Our Emcee TraeAnna Holiday, a true Seattleite, has watched her city change in many ways. She started training in media from the young age of 9 and continued through school up to Howard University and University of Washington. Through studying abroad multiple times, gaining her degree in Communications and Urban Studies, and being displaced by gentrification, she's now fueled to tell her neighborhood's stories through film and media. She found a home for this work at Converge Media as a two-time Emmy award winning Media Personality, TV Show Host and Producer, with her daily talk show, The Day With Trae, and other productions. As Communications Director for Washington state’s Office of Equity, she is able to bring her knowledge and enthusiasm for communications and the community to the state level. Flourishing with her style, passion, and drive, she dynamically creates impact media throughout her work, and helps the next generation find their voice in the world of media.  Our Keynote Speaker LaNesha McCoy is a skilled public historian with over 20 years experience leading African American museums and history initiatives. She is Executive Director of BlackPast.org, the world’s largest online encyclopedia on Black history which was founded by Dr. Quintard Taylor. Prior, she served as President & CEO of the Northwest African American Museum from 2017 to 2023 where she led strategic planning, recordsetting fundraising, and innovative programming. LaNesha is Past National President of the Association of African American Museums Board of Directors, only the sixth woman in history to serve in that capacity. A member of the International Council of Museums, she has studied museums globally in Kenya, Ghana, South Africa, England, Germany, and Israel. An ordained clergy, LaNesha is Assistant Pastor at the historic New Hope Missionary Baptist Church in Seattle's Central District and Executive Administrator of the New Hope Community Development Institute, which is focused on building affordable housing for people of color. Never forgetting her roots or the many mentors that shaped her life, she now has three master's degrees and is currently a PhD Candidate at the University of Washington where she teaches and researches African American educational history. She's a mentor and advocate, leading initiatives to support education.
